APPROPRIATENESS.justification: [Consensus avg=4] A(4): This paper falls squarely within top-tier NLP/ML conference scope: it tackles end-to-end neural dialogue agents with reinforcement learning for KB access, evaluates on multiple dataset sizes, compares sensible baselines (No-KB, Hard-KB, rule agents), and reports task success and reward metrics. From a reproducibility and experimental-rigor perspective there are some concerns: code release is not mentioned and the method relies on strong independence assumptions and specific belief-tracker designs, which should be ablated or better justified. Overall the work targets the right audience and uses expected methods and evaluations, but minor concerns about reproducibility and modeling assumptions prevent a perfect fit. | B(5): This paper sits squarely within top-tier NLP/ML venues: it addresses a central problem in dialogue systems (KB-grounded conversational search) and proposes a novel, differentiable Soft-KB lookup enabling end-to-end reinforcement learning of policies. The methodology (belief trackers, probabilistic posterior over KB rows, GRU policy trained with REINFORCE) and evaluation (task success, reward on multiple Movies-KB scales, comparisons to No-KB/Hard-KB/Rule baselines) meet community standards. While some assumptions (independent slots, no new-entity handling) limit scope, these are acknowledged and do not undermine the paper’s relevance to the venue’s audience focused on neural-symbolic integration and learning-based dialogue.

From a reproducibility and experimental-rigor perspective there are some concerns: code release is not mentioned and the method relies on strong independence assumptions and specific belief-tracker designs, which should be ablated or better justified. Overall the work targets the right audience and uses expected methods and evaluations, but minor concerns about reproducibility and modeling assumptions prevent a perfect fit.

reviewer_b.justification: This paper sits squarely within top-tier NLP/ML venues: it addresses a central problem in dialogue systems (KB-grounded conversational search) and proposes a novel, differentiable Soft-KB lookup enabling end-to-end reinforcement learning of policies. The methodology (belief trackers, probabilistic posterior over KB rows, GRU policy trained with REINFORCE) and evaluation (task success, reward on multiple Movies-KB scales, comparisons to No-KB/Hard-KB/Rule baselines) meet community standards. While some assumptions (independent slots, no new-entity handling) limit scope, these are acknowledged and do not undermine the paper’s relevance to the venue’s audience focused on neural-symbolic integration and learning-based dialogue.

CLARITY.justification: [Consensus avg=4] A(4): The paper is well-organized and presents contributions early (Soft-KB lookup for end-to-end training) with a clear method→experiments→conclusion flow; writing quality is reported as good and limitations are explicitly stated. Notation and probabilistic components (ptj, qtj, per-slot beliefs and KB posterior) are defined to support reproducibility and hyperparameters are reported, which aids clarity. Figures/tables and examples are described as effective, and related work coverage is adequate, though some sections (e.g., independence assumption and its implications) could be expanded for readers less familiar with KB-based dialogue. Overall the presentation is strong with only minor areas where additional exposition would help. | B(4): The paper is well-organized with a clear flow from motivation to method, experiments, and conclusions, and the main contributions (Soft-KB lookup and end-to-end RL training) are stated early. Notation for per-slot beliefs and the Soft-KB posterior appears consistent and well-defined, and the experiments/tables (different KB sizes, baselines, metrics) effectively support the claims. Writing quality is generally good, though a few sections (e.g., details of the LU and belief tracker architectures and the independence assumption) could be expanded for clarity. Overall presentation is strong with only minor areas where additional explanation would aid reproducibility and reader understanding.

MEANINGFUL_COMPARISON.justification: [Consensus avg=4] A(4): The paper evaluates against reasonable baselines (No-KB, Hard-KB, and hand-crafted Rule agents) on the same Movies-KB splits using identical task success and reward metrics, which supports fair comparisons. The authors clearly analyze the methodological differences (Soft-KB posterior vs. symbolic lookup) and report hyperparameters; related work coverage is described as adequate. However, the paper does not mention code release and it’s unclear whether all recent competing neural KB-access approaches were included, so the comparisons are strong but have minor omissions. | B(4): The paper evaluates Soft-KB against sensible baselines (No-KB entropy heuristic, symbolic Hard-KB lookup, and hand-crafted Rule agents) on the same Movies-KB splits using consistent metrics (success rate and reward), which indicates fair comparisons. The authors analyze how Soft-KB yields richer information from the LU/belief tracker versus Hard-KB and provide ablations (hand-crafted vs neural trackers, imitation learning), and their related-work discussion is described as adequate. However, the summary does not indicate inclusion of some more recent neural or differentiable KB-retrieval baselines or explicit acknowledgement when prior methods match performance, so the comparisons are strong but not fully comprehensive.

reviewer_a.justification: The paper evaluates against reasonable baselines (No-KB, Hard-KB, and hand-crafted Rule agents) on the same Movies-KB splits using identical task success and reward metrics, which supports fair comparisons. The authors clearly analyze the methodological differences (Soft-KB posterior vs. symbolic lookup) and report hyperparameters; related work coverage is described as adequate. However, the paper does not mention code release and it’s unclear whether all recent competing neural KB-access approaches were included, so the comparisons are strong but have minor omissions.

reviewer_b.justification: The paper evaluates Soft-KB against sensible baselines (No-KB entropy heuristic, symbolic Hard-KB lookup, and hand-crafted Rule agents) on the same Movies-KB splits using consistent metrics (success rate and reward), which indicates fair comparisons. The authors analyze how Soft-KB yields richer information from the LU/belief tracker versus Hard-KB and provide ablations (hand-crafted vs neural trackers, imitation learning), and their related-work discussion is described as adequate. However, the summary does not indicate inclusion of some more recent neural or differentiable KB-retrieval baselines or explicit acknowledgement when prior methods match performance, so the comparisons are strong but not fully comprehensive.

IMPACT.justification: [Consensus avg=4] A(4): The paper tackles an important practical problem—making KB retrieval differentiable to enable end-to-end RL training of dialogue agents—which is likely to change how researchers design neural InfoBots by replacing symbolic lookups with a Soft-KB posterior and showing empirical gains in task success and reward. Its integration of Soft-KB with policy learning and demonstration that a fully neural agent can be trained from user feedback opens a clear new research direction. However, the strong independence assumption across slots, limited scope (Movies-KB) and lack of mention of released code moderate its immediate transformative potential, making the impact strong but not yet field-changing. | B(4): The paper tackles an important practical problem—end-to-end training of dialogue agents that access structured KBs—by introducing a Soft-KB lookup that makes retrieval differentiable and enabling full RL training of KB-InfoBots. Empirically this yields higher task success and reward versus Hard-KB and No-KB baselines and therefore can change how researchers design neural dialogue agents by favoring differentiable KB interfaces. The work opens clear new research directions (scaling to realistic KBs, relaxing the independence assumption, handling novel entities) and is likely to be built upon, though its impact is somewhat constrained by the stated assumptions and current LU/belief-tracker limits.

reviewer_a.justification: The paper tackles an important practical problem—making KB retrieval differentiable to enable end-to-end RL training of dialogue agents—which is likely to change how researchers design neural InfoBots by replacing symbolic lookups with a Soft-KB posterior and showing empirical gains in task success and reward. Its integration of Soft-KB with policy learning and demonstration that a fully neural agent can be trained from user feedback opens a clear new research direction. However, the strong independence assumption across slots, limited scope (Movies-KB) and lack of mention of released code moderate its immediate transformative potential, making the impact strong but not yet field-changing.

reviewer_b.justification: The paper tackles an important practical problem—end-to-end training of dialogue agents that access structured KBs—by introducing a Soft-KB lookup that makes retrieval differentiable and enabling full RL training of KB-InfoBots. Empirically this yields higher task success and reward versus Hard-KB and No-KB baselines and therefore can change how researchers design neural dialogue agents by favoring differentiable KB interfaces. The work opens clear new research directions (scaling to realistic KBs, relaxing the independence assumption, handling novel entities) and is likely to be built upon, though its impact is somewhat constrained by the stated assumptions and current LU/belief-tracker limits.

ORIGINALITY.justification: [Consensus avg=4] A(4): The paper introduces a clearly new operational element — a differentiable Soft-KB posterior that replaces symbolic hard queries and enables end-to-end RL training of KB-InfoBots — which is a notable methodological departure from prior hard-retrieval or no-KB approaches. The contribution is well-differentiated from baselines (No-KB, Hard-KB, rule agents) and integrates probabilistic per-slot beliefs with policy learning in a non-trivial way. From a reproducibility-engineer perspective this is a meaningful novelty that would inform experts, though it is an incremental advance rather than a foundational paradigm shift given the strong independence assumptions and limited scope (Movies-KB). | B(4): The paper proposes a distinctive Soft-KB lookup—replacing symbolic hard queries with a differentiable posterior over KB entities—to enable end-to-end reinforcement learning of KB-InfoBots, which is a notable methodological contribution beyond standard hard-query pipelines. Framing KB retrieval as a probabilistic, differentiable operation integrated with belief trackers and neural policies (trained with REINFORCE and optional imitation) represents a non-trivial extension over prior dialogue agents that separate symbolic retrieval and policy learning. An expert would find this approach a clear and useful departure from prior hard-KB or No-KB baselines, though it builds on well-studied components (belief tracking, policy gradient), so it is more notable than revolutionary.

reviewer_a.justification: The paper introduces a clearly new operational element — a differentiable Soft-KB posterior that replaces symbolic hard queries and enables end-to-end RL training of KB-InfoBots — which is a notable methodological departure from prior hard-retrieval or no-KB approaches. The contribution is well-differentiated from baselines (No-KB, Hard-KB, rule agents) and integrates probabilistic per-slot beliefs with policy learning in a non-trivial way. From a reproducibility-engineer perspective this is a meaningful novelty that would inform experts, though it is an incremental advance rather than a foundational paradigm shift given the strong independence assumptions and limited scope (Movies-KB).

reviewer_b.justification: The paper proposes a distinctive Soft-KB lookup—replacing symbolic hard queries with a differentiable posterior over KB entities—to enable end-to-end reinforcement learning of KB-InfoBots, which is a notable methodological contribution beyond standard hard-query pipelines. Framing KB retrieval as a probabilistic, differentiable operation integrated with belief trackers and neural policies (trained with REINFORCE and optional imitation) represents a non-trivial extension over prior dialogue agents that separate symbolic retrieval and policy learning. An expert would find this approach a clear and useful departure from prior hard-KB or No-KB baselines, though it builds on well-studied components (belief tracking, policy gradient), so it is more notable than revolutionary.

SUBSTANCE.justification: [Consensus avg=4] A(3): The experiments provide reasonable controlled comparisons (No-KB, Hard-KB, Rule agents) across several dataset sizes and report task success and reward, which supports the core claim that Soft-KB helps policy learning. However, the summary suggests limited ablation/sensitivity analyses (e.g., impact of the independence assumption, multiple random seeds and variance, and robustness to LU/belief tracker architectures) and no mention of code release or statistical significance testing, which hampers reproducibility and confidence in gains. Related work and some limitations are discussed, but important empirical checks (repeats, error analyses, and stress tests for violated assumptions) appear missing. | B(4): The experiments are reasonably thorough for supporting the core claims: they evaluate Soft-KB against No-KB, Hard-KB and rule baselines across multiple Movie-KB sizes and report task success and reward, demonstrating benefits of the differentiable lookup. Ablation-like comparisons are present (Soft vs Hard vs No-KB), but deeper sensitivity analyses (e.g., varying slot independence violations, LU/belief tracker architectures, or real-user evaluations) are missing. Related work appears adequately covered and several limitations (column-independence assumption, inability to handle new entities/relations) are explicitly discussed; hyperparameters are reported though code availability is not mentioned, which slightly weakens reproducibility. Overall the empirical evaluation is solid with some important practical and robustness analyses left for future work.

reviewer_a.justification: The experiments provide reasonable controlled comparisons (No-KB, Hard-KB, Rule agents) across several dataset sizes and report task success and reward, which supports the core claim that Soft-KB helps policy learning. However, the summary suggests limited ablation/sensitivity analyses (e.g., impact of the independence assumption, multiple random seeds and variance, and robustness to LU/belief tracker architectures) and no mention of code release or statistical significance testing, which hampers reproducibility and confidence in gains. Related work and some limitations are discussed, but important empirical checks (repeats, error analyses, and stress tests for violated assumptions) appear missing.

reviewer_b.justification: The experiments are reasonably thorough for supporting the core claims: they evaluate Soft-KB against No-KB, Hard-KB and rule baselines across multiple Movie-KB sizes and report task success and reward, demonstrating benefits of the differentiable lookup. Ablation-like comparisons are present (Soft vs Hard vs No-KB), but deeper sensitivity analyses (e.g., varying slot independence violations, LU/belief tracker architectures, or real-user evaluations) are missing. Related work appears adequately covered and several limitations (column-independence assumption, inability to handle new entities/relations) are explicitly discussed; hyperparameters are reported though code availability is not mentioned, which slightly weakens reproducibility. Overall the empirical evaluation is solid with some important practical and robustness analyses left for future work.

SOUNDNESS_CORRECTNESS.justification: [Consensus avg=4] A(4): The paper clearly states its main claims and supports them with experiments comparing Soft-KB to No-KB, Hard-KB and rule baselines across multiple KB sizes, and reports task success and reward; mathematical formulation of the Soft-KB posterior appears consistent with the described tracker outputs. However, the summary lacks evidence of statistical rigor (no mention of multiple runs, variance or significance testing) and code release, which hampers reproducibility despite reported hyperparameters. The authors acknowledge a strong independence assumption and other limitations, so while the core experimental methodology and baselines are reasonable, missing experimental controls and reproducibility artifacts justify a minor deduction. | B(4): The paper clearly states its main claims and supports them with simulations showing higher task success and reward for Soft-KB versus No-KB and Hard-KB and compares to reasonable baselines (rule agents). The probabilistic Soft-KB formulation and its integration with REINFORCE appear mathematically coherent, but the strong independence assumption across slots is a notable unsupported simplification that could affect real-world applicability. Experimental methodology is generally sound (appropriate baselines and multiple KB sizes), though the summary lacks mention of statistical significance testing and human-user evaluation, limiting claims about deployed performance. Overall the technical work is solid with minor issues that do not undermine the main conclusions.

reviewer_a.justification: The paper clearly states its main claims and supports them with experiments comparing Soft-KB to No-KB, Hard-KB and rule baselines across multiple KB sizes, and reports task success and reward; mathematical formulation of the Soft-KB posterior appears consistent with the described tracker outputs. However, the summary lacks evidence of statistical rigor (no mention of multiple runs, variance or significance testing) and code release, which hampers reproducibility despite reported hyperparameters. The authors acknowledge a strong independence assumption and other limitations, so while the core experimental methodology and baselines are reasonable, missing experimental controls and reproducibility artifacts justify a minor deduction.

reviewer_b.justification: The paper clearly states its main claims and supports them with simulations showing higher task success and reward for Soft-KB versus No-KB and Hard-KB and compares to reasonable baselines (rule agents). The probabilistic Soft-KB formulation and its integration with REINFORCE appear mathematically coherent, but the strong independence assumption across slots is a notable unsupported simplification that could affect real-world applicability. Experimental methodology is generally sound (appropriate baselines and multiple KB sizes), though the summary lacks mention of statistical significance testing and human-user evaluation, limiting claims about deployed performance. Overall the technical work is solid with minor issues that do not undermine the main conclusions.

main_claims: Introduce a Soft-KB lookup that replaces symbolic (hard) queries with a differentiable posterior over KB entities to enable end-to-end training of KB-InfoBots.
Show that the Soft-KB lookup (versus Hard-KB and No-KB) helps the agent discover better dialogue policies by providing richer information from the language understanding (LU) unit.

Integrate the Soft-KB retrieval with reinforcement learning to obtain higher task success rate and reward in simulations and to enable a fully neural end-to-end agent trained entirely from user feedback.
_summary.methodology: Maintain per-slot belief trackers (hand-crafted or neural) producing slot value distributions ptj and known-value probabilities qtj, combine these via a probabilistic (Soft-KB) posterior over KB rows, and train a neural policy (GRU + softmax) with REINFORCE (and optional imitation learning) to select dialogue actions and sample KB results from the posterior.
datasets: Movies-KB (four splits: Small, Medium, Large, X-Large)
baselines: Rule agents (hand-crafted belief tracker + hand-crafted policy)
metrics: reward (including final rank-based reward and per-turn penalty)
limitations: Assumes independence of column values across slots (explicitly stated as a strong assumption).
Does not handle new entities or relations at test time (explicitly stated).
Authors note current limitations in the LU and Belief Tracker architecture and plan to employ better architectures / pretraining in future.
_summary.topic_area: NLP - dialogue systems / KB-based information access

reviewer_a.name: Methodical Reproducibility Engineer
reviewer_a.background: ML engineer with 8+ years building and evaluating dialogue systems and applied reinforcement learning models in industry and academia.
traits: insists on clear baselines, ablation studies, and controlled comparisons to justify each claimed improvement
looks for statistical rigor: multiple runs, variance reporting, significance tests or confidence intervals
values reproducibility: explicit hyperparameters, training details, dataset splits, and code/data release
checks experimental realism: whether simulations match user behavior and whether user studies are properly designed
reviewer_a.reviewing_style: Focuses on the experimental setup and empirical claims, demanding thorough ablations, reproducibility details, and rigorous evaluation before accepting results.

reviewer_b.name: Dialogue Systems Impact Scholar
reviewer_b.background: Senior researcher in conversational AI and knowledge-grounded dialogue with a track record of work on KB integration and end-to-end neural dialogue models.
traits: evaluates novelty and positioning relative to prior work on KB retrieval, differentiable querying, and neural-symbolic hybrids
values clarity about practical impact: scalability to realistic KBs, generalization across domains, and deployment considerations
notices conceptual contributions and their potential to shift research direction or enable new applications
considers limitations, failure modes, and whether the proposed approach meaningfully advances user-facing dialogue capabilities
reviewer_b.reviewing_style: Assesses the paper through the lens of novelty, significance, and real-world relevance, weighing how the idea integrates with and advances the broader research landscape.
